item.title: Treyarch studio head steps down after 22 years
item.content:encoded:
Mark Gordon, the studio head at Call of Duty maker Treyarch, has retired from his role after a 22 year tenure.

The studio shared the news yesterday via social media. In the statement, Treyarch highlights Gordon's contributions to the franchise, which involved the likes of Call of Duty 2: Big Red One, Call of Duty 3, World at War, and the entirety of the Black Ops series.

As for the studio's leadership, Treyarch's Kevin Hendrickson and Yale Miller will step up as co-studio heads.

"Kevin and Yale are veterans of the franchise with decades of development and leadership experience, both bringing a wealth of deep game knowledge and a shared commitment to Treyarch's culture and creative ambition," reads the statement.

Treyarch has been under Activision for over two decades. In 2023, Activision Blizzard was purchased by Microsoft for approximately $69 billion. During the last couple of years, the studio has been at the center of reports about crunch and culture issues, with the QA department reportedly bearing the brunt of it all. In late 2021, QA employees at the studio joined Raven Software and other devs in a walkout over the sudden firings at Raven.

Related:Banned horror title Horses begins recouping development costs after selling 30,000 copies

Treyarch has also seen some drama with studio leadership. In 2020, co-studio head Jason Blundell parted ways with the studio after 13 years. Then, five years later, co-founder Peter T. Akemann pled guilty and took a deal after piloting a drone during the Los Angeles fires in January, which damaged a firefighting aircraft.

All the while, Activision continues to pour ridiculous amounts of money in the Call of Duty franchise. In 2025, court filings unveiled that the company spent almost $1.8 billion developing three titles of the series. Yet, by the end of 2025, shortly after Activision announced a partnership with Paramount to develop a live action Call of Duty film, staff acknowledged the franchise has not met people's expectations fully, ditching back-to-back releases of Modern Warfare and Black Ops games. Now, the plan is to drive "innovation that is meaningful, not incremental."

2026 has been similarly tumultuous. Activision shut down Call of Duty: Warzone Mobile, while just last week, Xbox CEO Asha Sharma wasn't able to provide an affirmative answer regarding whether the $68.7 billion merger between Microsoft and Activision represented good value for money.

"I don't know anybody in entertainment who wouldn't want Call of Duty, which is now grossing in more revenue than [the] Marvel Cinematic Universe; who wouldn't want one of the top three apps in the world in Candy Crush; who wouldn't want World of Warcraft; who wouldn't want a team that for 20 years has been able to create predictable hits every single year," said Sharma.

Related:UK social media ban will restrict 'high-risk' features on game platforms

Now, just a few days after, multiple Xbox game studio heads are reportedly negotiating with Xbox to avoid closure, including Double Fine, Ninja Theory, and Compulsion Games.

About the Author

Contributing Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Diego Nicolás Argüello is a freelance journalist and critic from Argentina. Video games helped him to learn English, so now he covers them for places like The New York Times, NPR, Rolling Stone, and more. He also runs Into the Spine, a site dedicated to fostering and supporting new writers, and co-hosted Turnabout Breakdown, a podcast about the Ace Attorney series. He’s most likely playing a rhythm game as you read this.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/treyarch-studio-head-steps-down-after-22-years

item.title: EA Sports FC 26 and Call of Duty: Vanguard Lead the Back-Half of June 2026’s Xbox Game Pass Games
item.content:encoded:
Xbox has announced the second batch of Xbox Game Pass games that'll be joining its subscription service for the rest of June 2026. This second batch is meaningfully smaller than the first, though it still has a couple of big-name franchises attached. Like EA Sports FC 26, which heads to the service fresh off its extra stint on PS Plus Essential, to keep the world's biggest football game in front of as many players during football's biggest event, the FIFA World Cup.

The other notable headliner is Call of Duty: Vanguard, which feels like a soft launch for Xbox's reported plans to add older Call of Duty titles to Game Pass now that we won't be getting the brand-new releases into Xbox Game Pass Ultimate. As for the other five games either entering or moving between tiers in the service between today and the opening days of July, indie sensation RV There Yet? from last year's whirlwind of hit indie releases is more than worth checking out.

As is Tony Hawk's Pro Skater 3+4, which is the one game that's simply jumping from one tier to another, as players subscribed to the second-rung Premium tier will now be able to play it.

Here's the full list of games, when they arrive, what platforms you can find them and what tiers can access them:

  • Junkster - June 16 - Cloud, PC, Xbox Series X/S - PC Game Pass, Game Pass Ultimate
  • Call of Duty: Vanguard - June 17 - Cloud, PC, Console - Game Pass Premium, Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass
  • EA Sports FC 26 - June 18 - Cloud, PC, Console - Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass
  • Abyssus - June 25 - Cloud, PC, Xbox Series X/S - Game Pass Premium, Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass
  • RV There Yet? - June 30 - Cloud, PC, Xbox Series X/S - Game Pass Premium, Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass
  • Tony Hawk's Pro Skater 3+4 - July 2 - Cloud, PC, Console - Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass, now also on Game Pass Premium
  • Winds of Arcana: Ruination - July 6 - Cloud, PC, Console - Game Pass Premium, Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass

It's a smaller batch than what started the month with games like Persona 5 Royal, Undisputed, and Total Chaos thrown into the service, though it's doubtful that Xbox will catch slack for a lax Game Pass month when there are much bigger fish to fry if you've a bone to pick with Team Green.

Right now, the entire industry is waiting to find out just how terrible the reported mass layoffs at Xbox will be. We know of at least two studios that are heading for closure if they can't negotiate a buyout or find a new parent company, and we know that several more are on alert that they may also be on the chopping block.

Even if there's no threat of closure, the threat of mass layoffs and project cancellations apply across the entire Xbox Games Studios portfolio. After such a high of celebrating what's coming next in the industry with all of the announcements from Summer Game Fest, the rest of June is set to end on an incredibly low, sour note for the entire industry.

About the author: David has been writing about videogames, technology, and culture since 2020, with a focus on reporting daily news across multiple publications, including GameDaily.Biz, GameSkinny, and PlayStation Universe before joining Wccftech in 2025. David started contributing as Canada/US reporter for Wccftech's gaming section in 2025. Besides being up-to-date on the industry's movements, he loves interviewing developers, reviewing games, and writing intricate essays about the symbolism and layered meanings to be found in rich narratives as he's done for publications like GamesIndustry.Biz, LostInCult, and others. Outside of games he loves movies, music, theatre, his hometown, and his family, though not necessarily in that order.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/xbox-game-pass-june-2026-games-ea-sports-fc-26-call-of-duty-vanguard/

item.title: Jynxzi Couldn’t Save LCS Spring 2026 From Record Low Viewership
item.content:encoded:
The League of Legends Championship Series (LCS) drew the North American league’s lowest-ever viewership in Spring 2026.

The LCS Spring 2026 regular season attracted a new low of 86k viewers as interest in the LoL league dropped.

Now, according to Esports Charts, the LCS Spring 2026 Playoffs only peaked at 183,152 live viewers, an all-time low for an LCS split.

The peak viewership came during the LCS Playoffs final as LYON swept Team Liquid in three straight games.

If you haven’t already, download the free Insider Gaming App for the cleanest way to read your gaming news on iOS and Android:

  • Android Link: https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.insidergaming.app
  • iOS Link: https://apps.apple.com/us/app/insider-gaming/id6753984648

Jynxzi Fails to Have an Impact

And this was despite the fact that popular streamer Nicholas ‘Jynxzi’ Stewart co-streamed the final between LYON and Team Liquid.

Earlier this year, Jynxzi hosted a content creator LoL tournament with over 900k viewers, one of the most-watched LoL events this year.

In comparison, his co-stream of the LCS finals appears to have peaked at only 44,416.

Though, that comparison may be unfair considering the content creator event benefitted from various streamer’s audiences and the free Twitch Drops.

LCS Spring 2026 Flops

Surprisingly, the LCS Spring Split Playoffs’ peak audience of just over 183k was not NA’s biggest audience in 2026.

The LYON and Team Liquid final just barely failed to beat the LCS Lock-In, which peaked at 184,969 viewers during LYON vs Cloud9.

It seems the continued failures of Cloud9, one of the LCS’s most decorated a popular teams, is hurting the league.

Related News: Fnatic CEO confirms there have been acquisition bids but claims the brand “isn’t going anywhere.”

Before this year, no LCS split had failed to crack the 200,000 viewers milestone.

There was also an alarming drop off in average viewership this split.

LCS Spring averaged 63,949 viewers, while the Lock-In recorded 89,516 during the Lock-In, representing a drop of almost 29%.

What does the LCS need to do to make the league interesting again? Share your thoughts with us on the Insider Gaming Discord.

In other LCS-adjacent news, Team Liquid face a stern test as the MSI 2026 Play-In Bracket has been revealed.
